sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Functions in Kotlin are declared using the fun keyword. They are designed to be concise and expressive, often allowing you to skip curly braces and explicit return types.

sections.heading: Basic Function Anatomy
sections.content: A standard function includes a name, parameters with explicit types, and a return type.

sections.heading: Function Components
items: Parameters must always have explicit types (e.g., a: Int).
Return type is placed after the parameter list using a colon.
If a function returns nothing, the return type is Unit (similar to void in Java) and is usually omitted.

sections.heading: Single-Expression Functions
sections.content: If a function returns a single expression, you can omit curly braces and the return keyword. The compiler automatically infers the return type.
sections.code: fun multiply(a: Int, b: Int) = a * b

sections.heading: Default Arguments
sections.content: Kotlin allows you to provide default values for parameters. This reduces the need for multiple overloaded functions.

sections.heading: Named Arguments
sections.content: When calling a function, you can specify parameter names to improve readability or skip parameters with default values.

sections.heading: Function Scope
sections.content: Functions in Kotlin can exist in different scopes, not just inside classes.

sections.heading: Types of Functions
items: Top-level functions: Declared outside any class and globally accessible.
Member functions: Declared inside a class or object.
Local functions: Declared inside another function.

sections.heading: Local Function Example
sections.content: A local function is declared inside another function and is only accessible within that function.

sections.heading: Varargs (Variable Number of Arguments)
sections.content: If you don't know how many arguments will be passed to a function, use the vararg modifier.

sections.heading: Summary
items: vararg allows passing a variable number of arguments.
